analysis: Across the top candidates the pipeline is very consistent: a Pareto/nondominated prefilter → cheap per-point proxy ranking (box-volume and weight‑vector scalarizations) → small deterministic shortlist → a greedy/CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs → bounded 1‑for‑1 local swaps and deterministic padding. Stronger variants (1–5, 7–9) balance shortlist size against exact HV calls, add pairwise caches and timestamped CELF to avoid recomputing gains, and use careful deterministic tie‑breaking. Weaker entries (6, 10, 15) either make heavy/unbounded HV calls, use naive Python loops with randomization, or overuse very dense weight sets (memory/CPU overhead) without sufficient pruning. Common avoidable overheads: repeated pg.hypervolume instantiation and full-stack vstacking per candidate, membership tests (lists/sets) in hot loops, non‑vectorized nondominated checks, and insufficient HV‑call budgets or pruning thresholds. Best practices seen: singleton and selective pairwise caching, CELF timestamping, box‑volume upper bounds to prune, shortlist sized proportional to k and pareto size, and deterministic seeds/tie‑breakers for reproducibility.
experience: Prefer a small, k‑proportional shortlist driven by mixed box+few‑weight scores; use CELF with singleton (and selective pairwise) caching plus timestamping; prune candidates via box‑volume upper bounds and cap HV calls; vectorize nondominated filtering and hotspot ops; avoid dense weight banks—use adaptive sampling; and ensure deterministic tie‑breaks. These keep HV calls low while preserving solution quality.
current_reflection: Prefer a small, k‑proportional shortlist driven by mixed box+few‑weight scores; use CELF with singleton (and selective pairwise) caching plus timestamping; prune candidates via box‑volume upper bounds and cap HV calls; vectorize nondominated filtering and hotspot ops; avoid dense weight banks—use adaptive sampling; and ensure deterministic tie‑breaks. These keep HV calls low while preserving solution quality.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Make a tight shortlist (k‑proportional), cache singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs only when shortlist ≤ ~40, use box‑volume upper bounds to skip candidates, run CELF lazy greedy to minimize recomputations, cap HV calls and swaps, avoid costly per‑candidate array copies, and tune weight‑vector count adaptively. Deterministic seeds and set membership for O(1) checks improve reproducibility and speed.

Favor a compact, Pareto-aware shortlist (O(k)), CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching, and very aggressive cheap upper‑bound pruning (box- and scalar-based) to avoid HV calls. Use vectorized nondominated filtering, sets/heap for O(1)/O(log n) ops, an HV-call budget, and incremental swaps restricted to top candidates. Keep determinism by score-based tie-breaks; only inject controlled randomness if necessary.

Design pipelines that short‑circuit expensive HV calls: vectorize Pareto and proxy scoring, shortlist O(k) candidates, run CELF with singleton (and selective pairwise) caches, prune with box‑volume upper bounds, and limit exact HV swaps with a small deterministic budget. This yields reproducible, fast, and high‑quality k‑subsets without exploding HV computations.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: shortlist O(k), mixed box+few-weight scoring, CELF lazy greedy, singleton+selective pairwise caching, timestamps, box-volume bounds, HV budget, vectorized nondominated ops, adaptive sampling, deterministic ties.  
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points; score with mixed proxies and take a small k-proportional shortlist; run CELF with singleton and selective pairwise cached HVC deltas + timestamps; prune via box-volume upper bounds and an HV-call cap; vectorize nondominated/hotspot ops; use adaptive few-weight sampling; seed tie-breaks and return exactly k.  
- Avoid: dense weight banks, per-candidate array copies, nested/uncapped HV calls, nondeterministic tie-breaking, caching beyond shortlist utility.  
- Explanation: Cuts exact HV evaluations, concentrates effort on promising swaps, preserves solution quality and reproducibility.
